content:
Hi,Cronk While there is no single "magic button" to fix this specific cursor offset bug, the behavior you’re describing where the cursor jumps several characters to the left after deleting highlighted text is a known intermittent issue often linked to rendering glitches or hardware acceleration conflicts between Thunderbird and your OS. Because you are running a very recent (potentially beta/daily) version like 147, you are more susceptible to regressions in the underlying Gecko rendering engine. Potential Solutions and Workarounds

Disable Hardware Acceleration: This is the most common cause of "phantom" cursor positioning. Go to Settings > General. Scroll down to Indexing or search for "Performance". Uncheck Use hardware acceleration when available. Restart Thunderbird and see if the frequency of the "jump" decreases. 

Test in Troubleshoot Mode: To rule out a specific add-on or a corrupted CSS theme causing layout miscalculations:

Go to Help > Troubleshoot Mode... (or hold Shift while starting the app). If the problem disappears, one of your extensions or a custom theme is likely the culprit. Check "Paragraph" vs. "Body Text": Thunderbird occasionally struggles with cursor placement when switching between HTML block types. Go to Settings > Composition. Toggle Use Paragraph format instead of Body Text by default. Some users find that sticking to "Body Text" reduces unexpected cursor behavior during edits. Window State Glitch: In some Linux and Windows environments, an "offset mouse/cursor" bug is triggered by specific window scaling. Workaround: Try maximizing the window if it’s currently windowed, or vice versa. Some users report this "resets" the coordinate mapping for the text area. Disable "Hide pointer while typing" (Windows specific): Go to your OS Mouse Settings > Additional Mouse Options. Under Pointer Options, uncheck Hide pointer while typing. This OS-level feature can sometimes conflict with Thunderbird's internal cursor tracking. Since you mentioned this happens specifically when highlighting multiple characters, it may be related to how Thunderbird calculates the "selection end" vs. the "insertion point" in its latest rendering builds.
creator: Miran.H

content:
noh.spam said
It seems to me that the account settings in Thunderbird 140.6.0esr (64-bit) have changed,

Nope

because without any supporting documentation in 'help' I can no longer see a way to enter a password.

See https://support.mozilla.org/en-US/kb/password-manager-remember-delete-change-tb which you will note has not been edited since September last year and that was a minor edit.

The current version is expanded from my original blog post in 2011 https://thunderbirdtweaks.blogspot.com/2011/07/pssswords.html with the addition of an option to edit a saved password. But I can not see there ever being a need to allow the user to enter a password there.

So you are looking for something that has really never existed. Thunderbird will ask you for a password if it needs one and offer to save it.

However just setting the authentication method to password does not guarantee you will be asked if you have other software on your system that consumes the connection attempt so the server never requests a password. Common causes of failures to request passwords are things like antivirus products and in your case they are also blocking access to localhost and the odd proxy type port numbers that Proton uses.

Port 1143 appears to be registered for use by informix to exchange data. See https://www.infomatrix.ro/ and https://isc.sans.edu/data/port/1143 Port 1025 is often used as an alternative SMTP port, but it has a past in the Windows RPC and the associated vulnerabilities and patches over the years so a problematic choice on most occasions. See https://www.pentestpad.com/port-exploit/port-1025-microsoft-rpc-microsoft-remote-procedure-call

I have two gmail accounts which are working but I also have a Protonmail account which has stopped working. The Protonmail Bridge show the following details for entry in the Thunderbird client's account settings: IMAP: Hostname (127.0.0.1), Port(1143), Username(withheld), Password(withheld), Security (STARTTLS) SMTP: Hostname (127.0.0.1), Port(1025), Username(withheld), Password(withheld), Security (STARTTLS) I can enter the following: Hostname, (127.0.0.1), Port(1025), Username(withheld), Connection Security(STARTTLS) & Authentication method (normal password) but nowhere to enter the actual password!

You enter it when you are asked for it. Always have. Thunderbird does ask you to enter it when you are first creating an account, simply to reduce the number of password requests as there may be many attempts to connect to the mail server in the setup process attempting to detect the servers settings.

How do I do this and where in the docs is this described, please Thanks

As you appear to be using Linux in one of the X11 version I will guess it is probably a Ubuntu derived distribution and will be using SNAP packages with their special sandbox built in. How that works with those ports, I have no idea. But many folk have issues with the SNAP versions and permissions and have reverted to DEB packages which just work.

Ubuntu derived distributions will also most likely have the Ports 1025 and 1143 closed. While the definition of localhost says that the transmission should never go to a physical adaptor, I could see it being bundled with the general software firewall rules. So have you checked your software firewall? Have you checked your installed version of Thunderbird has access to those ports through the snap sandbox.

Do you have development environments or server software like Apache installed that may by consuming port 1025 connections or even things like mailpit or mailcatcher?

content:
noh.spam said
I'm sure I'm not going mad/senile. I remember entering my Protonmail password!? Also Wayland/OpenSuSE Could my partial configuration of Nginx/LetsEncrypt/PiHole have a bearing?

As I said before when you configure your account initially you are asked for your password. Also as I said you were asked for it and if it is needed again, or if the saved password is unsuccessful in making a connection, you will be asked for it again. After that it is saved and you can access that from the password manager.

Why are you using encrypted communications on your own hardware? the data travels nowhere, but you are adding the overhead of encrypting the communications between two software items on your local machine. I am sure an argument can be made that it makes the interception of the communication harder, but in all honesty Thunderbird stores your mail as plain text, so it is some weird transitory level of security. However you will need to add an exception to the certificate in the certificate manager is you want to use it, or use a trusted certificate issuing authority to sign your certificates.

But you are at point 4 or 5 in the Proton setup https://proton.me/support/protonmail-bridge-clients-windows-thunderbird

The dialog is different but the root cause is largely the same. Use the certificate manager to ad an exception for the server. (search cert in settings)

content:
Thunderbird itself does not use AI. Moreover, Thunderbird is a local email application, meaning it installs and runs as a client on your device. It does not provide email addresses, nor the service to transmit emails. But Thunderbird can be used with your existing email address and service provider to send, receive, sort and search your email messages.

Wrt your concern about Gmail and AI, this is completely unrelated to the email client you use to access your Gmail account. It is of course up to you not to use Gmail as your email provider if you feel uncomfortable about what they are doing. For standard, free users, Google maintains that they do not train Gemini or other large language models on your email content. Features like Smart Compose or Automatic Tab Sorting do "read" your emails to work, but Google clarified that these are localized processes. Turning them on does not grant Google the right to use your emails for AI training. If you explicitly use Gemini Extensions (where you ask the AI to "find the receipt in my Gmail"), the specific prompts you type and the resulting interactions may be used to improve the service, but the rest of your inbox remains private.

Note, every email provider is scanning your messages for malware, a.k.a. they are all 'reading' your email anyway.

content:
Let's try an easy approach first, as it may work. If your backup is a copy of the appdata\roaming\thunderbird folder, just copy that thunderbird folder to overlay the existing appdata\roaming\thunderbird folder with thunderbird NOT RUNNING, and restart thunderbird.

OK, if all you have is a backup of the profile, here's another, low-tech, approach. This is a bit clumsy, but I don't know your comfort zone. Do this. - Delete the appdata\roaming\thunderbird folder - download thunderbird again from thunderbird.net and reinstall it - start thunderbird, skip the option for new email address, and click help>troubleshootinginfformation - scroll down to 'profile folder' and click 'open folder' - EXIT thunderbird <== IMPORTANT - you are now in the profile. Copy your backup profile folder's contents and paste to overlay all content here - restart thunderbird
creator: davidsk

content: My guess is that thunderbird created a new profile and that the addressbook is there, but in another profile. My assumption is that Thunderbird was already installed and that you were updating to a newer version. Try this: - click help>troubleshootinginformation - scroll down to 'profiles' and click 'about:profiles' - if there is more than one profile showing, click 'open folder' on the inactive one - now, the addressbook's name is abook.sqlite. do you see one there, that may have been updated recently? If so, that may be your missing addressbook. If so, then copy the abook.sqlite and history.sqlite files to a separate folder (This is temporary) - now, close windows file manager and you are back in thunderbird at the list of profiles. Now, click 'open folder' for the active profile, then EXIT THUNDERBIRD, now, paste the two copied addressbooks here, overlaying the current files. - now, restart thunderbird and your addressbook should be there.
creator: davidsk

content: My first suggestion is to check the filter log to see where everything is going. My second suggestion (which may be too drastic, depending on volume of your mail) is to rename the msgfilterrules.dat file with thunderbird not running and then run thunderbird without filters to see if the unwanted mail movement continues. If the messages that you didn't want to be filter are be handled properly, that confirms that there is a problem in the filter somewhere. The other option is to post screenshots of all filters, including details on the email addresses that are going wrong and to which folder. Doing that may not give you the response you need, but no one can advise without seeing the details. Good luck.
